refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 43 minutes downtime per month) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; automated logging of outage duration and root cause; statistical analysis of availability = (total_time - total_downtime) / total_time.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation network link shall maintain connectivity with <0.1% downtime.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate failover or safe-state procedures within defined latency.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status and availability metrics to operators and site managers.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant network paths (primary + backup cellular/satellite) with automatic failover logic and continuous link health monitoring.
DP-B: Deploy a single high-reliability network link (e.g., dedicated LTE or fiber) with preventive maintenance and SLA-backed service.
DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining dual-path redundancy, link health monitoring, operator notification, and graceful degradation to reduced-capability mode.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining dual-path redundancy (primary + backup network), continuous link health monitoring with sub-second detection, automated failover logic, operator notification via SafetyAlert, and graceful degradation to reduced-capability teleoperation mode when primary link fails.

potential_sources: DP-C couples teleoperation network architecture (Teleoperation module) with safety state management (Safety Services module) via SafetyAlert interface.
DP-C couples network redundancy strategy with operator UI/notification (Teleoperation module) and site-level monitoring (Safety Services module).
Coupling is acceptable because link availability is inherently a system-level concern requiring coordination across network, teleoperation, and safety domains.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is intentional and necessary; no decoupling recommended. The design trades off architectural simplicity for operational resilience, which aligns with high safety criticality.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Backup network (e.g., satellite or secondary cellular) is available and contractually guaranteed at the worksite.
Assumption: Failover latency (sub-second) is acceptable to operators and does not violate safety policies.
Risk: Backup network may have higher latency or lower bandwidth, degrading operator experience during failover.
Risk: Dual-path redundancy increases cost and complexity; customer may not accept the trade-off.
Risk: Link health monitoring logic must be robust to avoid false-positive failovers that disrupt operations.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) failover detection threshold (e.g., 3 consecutive missed heartbeats = 300 ms), (2) graceful degradation mode (e.g., reduced command frequency, telemetry sampling), (3) operator notification protocol (SafetyAlert + Tasking interface updates), and (4) recovery logic (automatic revert to primary when healthy).

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the teleoperation link availability architecture, including dual-path network management, link health monitoring, failover logic, operator notification, and graceful degradation. Interfaces with Safety Services for SafetyAlert notifications and with Vehicle for Tasking command delivery and Telemetry reception.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication channel (primary + backup paths) carrying command and telemetry data. Teleoperation module manages link state, redundancy, and failover.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module notifies Safety Services of link loss, failover events, and degraded-mode status to trigger operator alerts and safe-state procedures.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module sends operator commands to Vehicle via Tasking interface; failover may reduce command frequency during backup-link operation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state, sensor data, and link health metrics from Vehicle; used to assess link quality and inform failover decisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability logic, failover, and operator-facing notifications. High containment because dual-path redundancy, link health monitoring, and failover are core teleoperation concerns. Minor diffusion: Safety Services must implement safe-state procedures triggered by SafetyAlert.

Safety Services could own link availability as a safety-critical function, but this would diffuse network/failover logic (inherently teleoperation-specific) into a safety module. Not recommended; Safety Services should react to link-loss alerts, not own the link architecture.
Vehicle module should not own teleoperation link availability; this is a remote-operation concern, not a vehicle-local concern. High diffusion if attempted.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container but should not directly implement link availability logic; this is a delegated responsibility to Teleoperation module.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module owns dual-path network management, link health monitoring, failover logic, and operator notification. Safety Services owns safe-state procedures triggered by SafetyAlert. Vehicle owns command execution and telemetry generation. This allocation minimizes diffusion and aligns with architectural separation of concerns.
containment_risks: Risk: If backup network is managed by external provider (e.g., satellite operator), Teleoperation module must interface with external APIs; ensure contract and SLA clarity.
Risk: Link health monitoring logic must be robust and well-tested to avoid false failovers; recommend extensive simulation and field validation.
Risk: Failover latency and graceful degradation behavior must be validated against operator expectations and safety policies; recommend early stakeholder review.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the boundary between Teleoperation (link management, failover, operator notification) and Safety Services (safe-state procedures, operator alerts). Create a detailed interface specification for SafetyAlert to ensure Safety Services can respond appropriately to link-loss events. Specify backup network provisioning and SLA requirements as external constraints.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid redundancy + monitoring + failover) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve 0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. Dual-path redundancy and sub-second failover are proven approaches in safety-critical teleoperation systems. No decoupling issues identified.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion: Safety Services must implement safe-state procedures in response to SafetyAlert notifications from Teleoperation. This is acceptable because Safety Services owns safety logic, not network logic.
Minor diffusion: Vehicle module must provide Telemetry (link health metrics) to Teleoperation. This is acceptable because Vehicle is the source of telemetry data.
No high-diffusion risks identified. Teleoperation is the clear owner of link availability logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves 0.95 containment score. The DP is well-aligned with the module boundary. External interfaces (Network Link, SafetyAlert, Tasking, Telemetry) are all known and specified. No new interfaces required.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via continuous monitoring of link state during the evaluation period. Automated logging of outage events, failover triggers, and recovery times enables statistical analysis of availability. Recommend field trials with representative network conditions (e.g., simulated link loss, latency spikes).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns link availability architecture and measurement. Safety Services owns safe-state procedures. Vehicle owns telemetry generation. Site Manager and Tele-operator own operational procedures and SLA compliance monitoring.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify failover detection threshold, graceful degradation mode, and recovery logic (see DP section).
Define SafetyAlert interface specification to ensure Safety Services can respond appropriately to link-loss events.
Specify backup network provisioning, SLA requirements, and external provider contracts as constraints.
Plan field trials to validate failover latency, operator experience, and safety procedure effectiveness.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into three sub-FRs (network link reliability, failover detection/response, and link status monitoring). DP-C is sound and well-aligned with Teleoperation module, but further decomposition is required to detail failover logic, graceful degradation, and operator notification. Recommend proceeding to the next decomposition level for each sub-FR.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation network link shall maintain connectivity with <0.1% downtime (dual-path redundancy, network SLA management).
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall detect link loss and initiate failover or safe-state procedures within <500 ms (link health monitoring, failover logic).
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall provide real-time link status and availability metrics to operators and site managers (monitoring UI, SafetyAlert notifications).

assumptions: Backup network (e.g., satellite or secondary cellular) is available and contractually guaranteed at the worksite.
Failover latency <500 ms is acceptable to operators and does not violate safety policies.
Link health monitoring can reliably detect link loss within 300 ms (3 consecutive missed heartbeats).
Graceful degradation to reduced-capability mode is operationally acceptable during backup-link operation.
Safety Services can implement safe-state procedures in response to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable latency.
open_risks: Backup network may have higher latency or lower bandwidth, degrading operator experience during failover.
Dual-path redundancy increases cost and complexity; customer may not accept the trade-off.
Link health monitoring logic must be robust to avoid false-positive failovers that disrupt operations.
Failover and recovery behavior must be validated against operator expectations and safety policies.
External network provider SLA and contract terms must be clearly defined and monitored.
evidence_needed: Network architecture design document specifying primary and backup paths, failover logic, and SLA requirements.
Link health monitoring algorithm specification and validation results (simulation, lab testing).
Failover latency measurements from field trials under representative network conditions.
Operator feedback on failover experience and graceful degradation behavior.
Safety procedure validation confirming safe-state procedures are triggered appropriately by SafetyAlert.
Customer acceptance of dual-path redundancy approach and cost/complexity trade-offs.
